1996.assessment: Extreme press repression. Election fraud (1996-97) — media coverage manipulated. B92 temporarily shut down. Protests forced some concessions. Independent Radio Index founded. Regime used media as tool of election fraud.

1997.assessment: Extreme press repression. State media dominance. Independent outlets marginalized. Journalist organizations under pressure.

1998.assessment: Maximum repression. New Media Law (March 1998) — draconian censorship. B92 shut down permanently by order. Kosovo war reporting censored. Foreign journalists expelled. Worst press freedom in post-Yugoslav Serbia.

1999.assessment: NATO bombing + wartime = total information control. Foreign journalists expelled. State media only source. Journalists who reported on war crimes risked imprisonment. B92 operating from exile. Total press censorship during Kosovo War.

2000.assessment: Transitioning. Bulldozer Revolution (October 2000) — immediate media liberation. B92 returned from exile, expanded to TV. RTS (state TV) democratized. Independent media flourished overnight. From worst to rapidly improving.

2001.assessment: Dramatic improvement. Independent media flourishing. B92 expanded to national TV. Hundreds of new publications. Political pluralism. Milošević extradited — journalists free to report. Estimated RSF rank ~40-50.

2002.assessment: First RSF index year for Serbia. Strong press freedom. Independent media thriving. RTS reformed. Competitive media market. Free political reporting. PM Đinđić supported press freedom.

2003.assessment: Good press freedom. Đinđić assassination (March 2003) — media freely reported. State of emergency — brief restrictions on reporting. Independent media active. Decline from 2002 peak but still strong.

2004.assessment: Good press freedom. Competitive media market. Independent newspapers, TV stations. Judiciary beginning to protect press. EU accession process requiring press freedom standards.

2005.assessment: Good press freedom. Best period of press freedom in Serbia's modern history. Independent TV B92, RTV, Pink commercial. Newspapers competitive. Strong journalist associations.

2006.assessment: Good press freedom. Serbia independent. New constitution protects press freedom. EU SAA negotiations driving standards. Some concerns about tabloid growth (Kurir) with aggressive tactics.

2007.assessment: Good press freedom. Competitive elections. Media covered politics freely. Some media concentration concerns. Tabloids growing. EU SAA approaching.

2008.assessment: Good but declining. Kosovo independence — media covered freely though tensions. Media market concentration accelerating. Some advertising pressure on editorial independence. RSF rank slipping slightly.

2009.assessment: Declining. Media market consolidation. Pro-government Informer tabloid growing. Advertising pressure from state enterprises affecting editorial decisions. EU candidate process maintaining formal standards.

2010.assessment: Declining. Media concentration. State enterprises using advertising to influence coverage. Government officials pressuring owners. Press freedom still functional but weakening.

2011.assessment: Declining. Independent media under economic pressure. Tabloids dominating. Self-censorship increasing. Media law reforms discussed. EU candidate status requiring reform attention.

2012.assessment: Declining. SNS (Vučić) won elections. Concerns about media alignment with new government. EU candidate status. Tabloids (Informer, Srpski telegraf) growing and government-aligned.

2013.assessment: Significant decline. Vučić government pressure on media. SNS influence over editorial decisions through state advertising. Informer tabloid directly pro-Vučić. Self-censorship widespread. Journalists reporting threats.

2014.assessment: Declining. Vučić as PM. Media owners aligned with government. State advertising weaponized. Independent B92 TV changed ownership and editorial line. NUNS (journalist union) reported increased harassment.

2015.assessment: Declining. 5 of 6 national TV stations aligned with government. Informer/Srpski telegraf tabloids leading with pro-government content. Kurir tabloid also aligned. Independent journalism confined to online outlets (BIRN, CINS, N1).

2016.assessment: Further decline. Freedom House downgraded Serbia press freedom. Journalists facing lawsuits (SLAPPs). Government officials attacking critical journalists as 'foreign agents'. N1 TV (CNN affiliate) became primary independent broadcaster.

2017.assessment: Declining. Vučić became president. Media environment worsened. Pro-Vučić tabloids dominating newsstands. Journalists physically attacked at protests. Online threats against journalists. Independent outlets struggling economically.

2018.assessment: Continuing decline. 'Jedan od pet miliona' protests demanded free media. RSF: Serbia 76th — 'problematic situation'. Government calling critical media 'foreign servants'. Threats against journalists documented.

2019.assessment: Major decline. RSF rank 90 — 'difficult situation'. Media almost entirely captured. Most TV and print newspapers pro-government. Opposition boycotted elections — media coverage was main demand. BIRN/CINS/N1 as independent holdouts.

2020.assessment: Difficult situation. RSF rank 93. COVID-19 emergency — journalists detained for reporting 'fake news' about pandemic. State of emergency used to restrict press. Government controlling pandemic narrative. Opposition media boycotted elections.

2021.assessment: Difficult situation. RSF rank 93 stable. Media landscape: 5 national TV stations pro-government, only N1 and Nova S independent. Online threats against journalists documented. BIRN/CINS investigative journalism under legal pressure.

2022.assessment: Some methodological improvement in RSF scoring (new methodology). Rank 79. Russia-Ukraine war: Serbia's neutrality meant government-friendly framing of Russia. N1 and Nova S maintaining independent coverage. Investigative journalism active despite harassment.

2023.assessment: Declining. RSF rank 89. November 2023 elections — media coverage heavily biased to government. Protests afterwards demanding fair media. Journalists covering protests faced police aggression. BIRN journalists reporting harassment.

2024.assessment: Significant decline. RSF rank 98. Novi Sad railway station collapse (November 2024) — journalists covering protests attacked, equipment seized. Anti-government media facing coordinated online harassment campaigns. Worst RSF rank in Serbia's democratic era.

2025.assessment: Protests continuing. Press freedom under severe pressure. Uncertain trajectory.

1995.assessment: FRY statistical office undermined by sanctions, wars, and authoritarian control. No reliable national statistics. IMF/World Bank data collection blocked. Statistical transparency minimal.

1996.assessment: FRY statistical capacity impaired by isolation. Sanctions blocked IMF/World Bank data coordination.

1997.assessment: FRY statistical capacity limited. No integration with international statistical frameworks.

1998.assessment: FRY wartime. Statistical capacity severely compromised. Kosovo data unavailable. Economic statistics unreliable.

1999.assessment: Wartime. Statistical office non-functional for international purposes. Kosovo data unavailable.

2000.assessment: Revolution year. Statistical capacity reforms beginning. Serbia rejoined IMF/World Bank — data sharing requirements initiated.

2001.assessment: Serbia joined IMF and World Bank. Statistical office (RZS) reforms beginning. International standards adoption initiated.

2002.assessment: RZS (Statistical Office of Serbia) reforms advancing. EU/IMF standards being adopted. Preliminary data quality improvements.

2003.assessment: Last pre-WB SCI year. Statistical reforms continuing. Serbia & Montenegro created — separate statistical offices maintained.

2004.assessment: First WB SCI year. Serbia score 73.33/100 — moderate statistical capacity. Post-revolution statistical reforms showing results. Census conducted 2002 (population). EU accession driving statistical modernization.

2005.assessment: Improving. EU SAA negotiations requiring Eurostat-compatible statistics. SCI improving. GDP measurement improved. Labour Force Survey conducted regularly.

2006.assessment: Improving. Serbia independent (Montenegro June 2006). RZS as sole national statistical office. EU SAA signed. Eurostat partnership advancing. Statistical modernization program.

2007.assessment: Improving. Eurostat partnership. New Law on Statistics adopted. Statistical independence enshrined. RZS modernization program.

2008.assessment: Good statistical capacity. EU SAA signed. ESS (European Statistical System) observer status. GDP compilation according to ESA95. Price statistics improved.

2009.assessment: Good statistical capacity. EU membership application. Eurostat cooperation deepening. GFS (Government Finance Statistics) compliance improving.

2010.assessment: Good and improving. EU candidate process. ESA2010 preparation. Census 2011 prepared. Statistical capacity among better performers in Western Balkans.

2011.assessment: Good capacity. Census 2011 conducted. EU candidate status approved. Eurostat peer review. Statistical Law aligned with EU.

2012.assessment: Good stable capacity. EU candidate status. Census 2011 results published. National accounts revision. Statistical independence maintained despite Vučić election win.

2013.assessment: Good stable capacity. Brussels Agreement with Kosovo. EU accession advancing. Statistical office modernization continuing.

2014.assessment: Good stable capacity. EU accession negotiations opened. ESA2010 implementation. National accounts modernized. Statistical independence maintained despite political pressures.

2015.assessment: Good stable capacity. Eurostat cooperation. Statistical independence formalized. Among best statistical capacity in Western Balkans.

2016.assessment: Good stable capacity. Statistical office maintaining independence despite political environment.

2017.assessment: Good stable capacity. EU reporting requirements maintained. Labor force survey quality improving.

2018.assessment: Good stable capacity. Eurostat alignment maintained. Statistical independence formally maintained though political pressures on some public data releases.

2019.assessment: Good stable capacity. EU accession Chapter 18 (Statistics) well-advanced. Eurostat certifications.

2020.assessment: Good stable capacity. Final WB SCI year (WB discontinued SCI after 2020). COVID impacted some data collection. Census 2021 postponed. Statistical capacity maintained at 83.33/100.

2021.assessment: Estimated ~82/100 (WB SCI discontinued). Census 2021 conducted (postponed from 2020). Eurostat alignment maintained. Statistical independence under mild political pressure.

2022.assessment: Estimated ~81/100. Eurostat partnership maintained. EU accession Chapter 18 advancing. Some political pressure on statistical releases. Census 2021 results published.

2023.assessment: Estimated ~80/100. EU accession statistics chapter. Some concerns about selective data releases by government. Investigative journalists questioning unemployment and economic statistics.

2024.assessment: Estimated ~79/100. Mass protests demanding institutional accountability. Statistical office nominally independent but political environment affects releases. Eurostat cooperation maintained.

2025.assessment: Statistical capacity maintained at Eurostat standards formally. Political situation uncertain amid protests.
